stories.headline: Denver ordered to pay $14 million to George Floyd protesters for unconstitutional force
stories.summary:
The 10th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals ruled Tuesday that Denver must pay $14 million to 12 protesters injured by police during the 2020 George Floyd demonstrations. The court upheld a jury verdict finding that Denver Police Department officers and officers from other jurisdictions used "unconstitutional force" against peaceful protesters between May 28 and June 2, 2020, causing injuries ranging from tear gas inhalation to a fractured skull and broken neck.

The appeals court rejected Denver's arguments that the lower court made procedural errors during the 2022 trial, including allowing testimony from the city's independent monitor. The court upheld the verdict specifically on the grounds that Denver inadequately trained its officers, resulting in the civil rights violations.

The jury had originally awarded damages ranging from $750,000 to $3 million per plaintiff. Denver officials appealed the verdict, but the 10th Circuit denied all of the city's arguments. The ACLU of Colorado and attorneys for the plaintiffs called the ruling "monumental" and said it should serve as a warning to law enforcement agencies nationwide.

stories.headline: Judge blocks Gov. Polis from directing state officials to comply with ICE subpoena for second time
stories.summary:
Denver District Court Judge A. Bruce Jones on Tuesday again prohibited Gov. Jared Polis from ordering state Department of Labor and Employment officials to hand over records to federal immigration authorities about sponsors of immigrant children. The ruling marks the second time in 10 months that Jones has blocked Polis on this issue, finding that the new March 13 subpoena was substantially identical to one from last year and that complying would likely violate state law prohibiting information-sharing with federal immigration officials.

Jones specifically barred Polis from directing the Division of Labor Standards and Statistics to comply, but did not issue the sweeping injunction sought by attorneys representing a former state labor official and the state employees union. The judge acknowledged the new subpoena could not reasonably be viewed outside the context of the earlier legal battle, noting it involved the same people, same agency, and same apparent request.

In a statement, Polis spokesman Eric Maruyama said the governor would abide by the court's decision and was "grateful that this is a limited ruling." Polis had argued he could comply because the subpoena was part of a criminal investigation, which would be permissible under state law — a claim Judge Jones rejected both last year and again Tuesday.

stories.headline: Colorado Democrats introduce 'No Kings Act' allowing civil suits against public officials for rights violations
stories.summary:
With three weeks remaining in the legislative session, Democratic state Sens. Mike Weissman and Julie Gonzales have introduced the "No Kings Act," a bill that would allow Coloradans to file civil suits in state court against any public official they believe has violated their constitutional rights. Sponsors say the measure is aimed primarily at holding federal officials accountable, since federal courts provide broad immunity protections that state courts are not bound to follow.

Opponents, including the Colorado District Attorneys Council, argue that federal employees would largely be shielded anyway, and the bill would instead expose state and local officials — from the governor to teachers and librarians — to a flood of civil litigation. The DA's Council warned that governments would face costly settlements regardless of actual liability simply to avoid legal defense costs.

Sponsors counter that the bill does not create greater liability for state and local officials than already exists under federal law and specifically allows defendants to assert government immunity in state court. They say they were constitutionally required to include all public officials because the Supremacy Clause prevents them from targeting federal employees alone.

stories.headline: Xcel Energy expands preemptive power shutoff plans to western Colorado and San Luis Valley amid extreme wildfire risk
stories.summary:
Xcel Energy announced Tuesday it is planning Public Safety Power Shutoffs for customers in portions of Garfield, Mesa and Pitkin counties beginning around 9 a.m. Wednesday, and in Alamosa, Conejos, Costilla, Rio Grande and Saguache counties beginning around 11 a.m., due to extreme wildfire conditions. This would be the first time Xcel has enacted preemptive shutoffs in Western Slope counties; previous events have been limited to the Front Range and northern Colorado.

The utility cited a combination of strong winds, low humidity, record heat and exceptionally dry conditions driven by a record warm and dry winter. The National Weather Service has issued a Red Flag Warning for the San Luis Valley for Wednesday, and much of the region is rated in extreme to exceptional drought by the U.S. Drought Monitor. Conditions are expected to improve around 7 p.m., with restoration beginning after line inspections are completed — a process that could take several hours to several days in remote areas.

Xcel is also activating Enhanced Powerline Safety Settings statewide during the next 48 hours, which increase line sensitivity and can extend outage durations. The company is providing up to $10,000 in rebates for backup battery systems for customers who rely on medical devices and urged all affected customers to update contact information and prepare emergency kits.

stories.headline: Adams County deputies shoot armed man who emerged from home during search for stabbing suspect
stories.summary:
Adams County Sheriff's deputies shot a 32-year-old man early Tuesday morning after he came out of a home armed with a gun while deputies were searching the area for a stabbing suspect. The shooting occurred in the 6900 block of Dexter Street, about one-tenth of a mile from where the original stabbing was reported just after 1 a.m. near East 70th Avenue and Dexter Street.

Deputies gave the man commands before two of them opened fire, according to the sheriff's office. It remained unclear Tuesday whether the man had fired his weapon or whether he was connected to the stabbing that prompted the search.

The injured man was transported to a hospital with undisclosed injuries and had not been publicly identified. Both deputies involved were placed on administrative leave pending an investigation, which is standard procedure following officer-involved shootings. No deputies were injured in the incident.

stories.headline: Copper thieves steal wire from RTD G Line, briefly suspend service in Adams County
stories.summary:
Adams County Sheriff's Office deputies responded around 5:15 a.m. Tuesday to a report of copper wire theft from an RTD rail line, prompting a temporary suspension of G Line service. RTD did not publicly disclose the exact location of the theft at the time, but deputies set up a perimeter in an attempt to locate the suspects, who were not found.

The suspects made off with 14 feet of copper wire before fleeing the scene. G Line service was restored at 6:17 a.m. after RTD assessed the situation.

RTD police detectives are continuing to investigate the case. Copper theft from transit and utility infrastructure has been an ongoing concern for regional agencies, as stolen wiring can disrupt service and pose safety risks.

stories.headline: Denver development authority declines $29 million loan request for downtown office-to-apartment conversion
stories.summary:
The Denver Downtown Development Authority has rejected a $29 million loan application from Revesco Properties to convert the 16-story office building at 475 17th Street into 140 apartments — a project estimated to cost $77 million in total. Authority officials cited concerns about the total loan amount, the cost per unit of roughly $207,150, and the proposed 15-year repayment period, all of which were higher than benchmarks set by four previously approved conversion projects.

The DDDA, a city affiliate with up to $470 million in bond funding for downtown revitalization, has financed four other office-to-residential conversions at per-unit costs ranging from $78,650 to $146,550. Chief Projects Officer Bill Mosher said he was uncertain why this project's costs were so much higher. A loan for a separate 36-story hotel and apartment project on the former Greyhound block was also declined.

Revesco can revise its application and resubmit. The company is exploring low-income housing tax credits as a potential way to reduce the required loan amount. Office-to-residential conversions have been a stated priority for the DDDA as part of broader efforts to bring more residents to downtown Denver.

stories.headline: Law firm FBT Gibbons leases full floor at downtown Denver's 1801 California building
stories.summary:
FBT Gibbons, a law firm formed through a January merger, has signed a full-floor lease at 1801 California, one of downtown Denver's most prominent office towers. The lease expands the firm's Denver footprint and adds to a recent string of commercial real estate activity in the city's central business district.

The 1801 California building is a 56-story skyscraper that has long been a marquee address for major corporate and legal tenants in Denver. The deal reflects continued demand from professional services firms for high-profile downtown space even as the broader office market continues to face elevated vacancy rates.

Details on the size of the leased floor and the financial terms of the agreement were not publicly disclosed.

stories.headline: Timberwolves erase 19-point deficit to beat Nuggets 119-114, even series at 1-1
stories.summary:
The Minnesota Timberwolves rallied from a 19-point first-quarter hole to defeat the Denver Nuggets 119-114 in Game 2 of their first-round NBA playoff series Monday night at Ball Arena. Anthony Edwards led Minnesota with 30 points and 10 rebounds, while Julius Randle added 24 points. The Nuggets led 39-25 after the first quarter but allowed Minnesota to outscore them 39-25 in the second period, with the half ending in a tie at 64-64 after Jamal Murray hit a halfcourt buzzer-beater.

Nikola Jokic finished with 24 points but acknowledged after the game that he should have taken a floater with 21 seconds remaining rather than passing to Christian Braun in the dunker spot. Braun was fouled on the play and missed the first of two free throws, costing Denver a chance to tie the game at 115. Jokic and Murray combined to miss seven consecutive shots during clutch time as Minnesota closed out the victory.

The series is now tied 1-1, with Game 3 shifting to Target Center in Minneapolis on Thursday night. The Nuggets won Game 1 on Saturday.

stories.headline: Rockies claim right-handed pitcher Blas Castano off waivers from Seattle Mariners
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies claimed right-handed pitcher Blas Castano off waivers from the Seattle Mariners and optioned him to Triple-A Albuquerque, the team announced. In a corresponding move, left-handed pitcher Luis Peralta was designated for assignment.

Castano made one major league appearance with Seattle in 2025, allowing four hits and three earned runs over three innings before being sent down. He has performed well to open the 2026 minor league season, posting a 1.42 ERA and a 4-to-1 strikeout-to-walk ratio across six appearances.

Over 147 minor league games, Castano carries a 4.44 ERA with 513 strikeouts against 208 walks. Colorado's bullpen has been one of the stronger units in the majors early in 2026, ranking seventh in the league with a 3.49 ERA among relief pitchers.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers set January 11, 2027 as start date for next legislative session
stories.summary:
Legislative leaders confirmed Tuesday that the 2027 Colorado General Assembly will convene on Monday, January 11, a departure from the usual second-Wednesday-in-January start that is made necessary by the upcoming gubernatorial transition. The adjustment ensures election results, including the governor's race, are certified before the new administration is inaugurated.

Senate President James Coleman rejected an earlier proposed start date of January 8, citing the burden it would place on rural lawmakers who would have to travel to Denver for a single day before returning home. He specifically referenced Senate Minority Leader Cleave Simpson of Alamosa as an example.

With a 120-day calendar, the January 11 start places the final day of the session on May 10. Legislative leaders noted that recent sessions have routinely extended through final weekends, making the late-spring end date unlikely to cause significant disruption.

stories.headline: Panel recommends partial demolition of Denver Pavilions, residential towers on downtown parking lots
stories.summary:
A planning panel has recommended partially demolishing the Denver Pavilions retail complex and constructing residential towers on several surface parking lots in the downtown core. The recommendation is part of broader efforts to increase downtown Denver's residential population and address persistent vacancies in the retail corridor along the 16th Street Mall.

The specific scope of the partial demolition and the number of proposed residential units were not detailed in available reporting. The recommendation would need to advance through additional city review processes before any redevelopment could proceed.

The proposal reflects ongoing tensions in downtown Denver between preserving existing retail infrastructure and converting underutilized space to housing, as city officials continue to pursue strategies to revitalize the urban core following years of elevated office and retail vacancy.

weather.notable: About 20 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: Dogs are the leaders of the planet. If you see two life forms, one of them's making a poop, the other one's carrying it for him, who would you assume is in charge?

topStory.headline: Federal Grand Jury Indicts Southern Poverty Law Center on Fraud, Money Laundering Charges
topStory.summary:
The U.S. Department of Justice announced Tuesday that a federal grand jury in Montgomery, Alabama, has returned an 11-count indictment against the Southern Poverty Law Center, a prominent civil rights nonprofit based in Alabama. The charges include six counts of wire fraud, four counts of making false statements to a federally insured bank, and one count of conspiracy to commit money laundering. The indictment alleges the organization secretly funneled more than $3 million to paid informants affiliated with white supremacist and neo-Nazi groups — including the Ku Klux Klan, the Aryan Nations, the National Socialist Movement and the American Front — between 2014 and 2023.

Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, flanked by FBI Director Kash Patel at a press conference, said the SPLC used shell companies and fictitious entities to conceal the payments while simultaneously soliciting donations by claiming to dismantle extremist groups. "The SPLC was not dismantling these groups," Blanche said. "It was instead manufacturing the extremism it purports to oppose by paying sources to stoke racial hatred." Among the specific allegations, Blanche said the SPLC paid approximately $270,000 over eight years to a member of the leadership group that organized the 2017 Unite the Right rally in Charlottesville, Virginia, which resulted in one death and dozens of injuries.

SPLC interim Chief Executive Bryan Fair denied the allegations and said the organization's use of paid confidential informants was a standard and necessary practice to gather intelligence on violent extremist groups. "We are outraged by the false allegations levied against SPLC," Fair said in a statement. "Taking on violent hate and extremist groups is among the most dangerous work there is, and we believe it is also among the most important work we do. To be clear, this program saved lives." Earlier in the day, before the indictment was announced, Fair said in a video that the SPLC's informants had risked their lives to infiltrate and report on the nation's most dangerous extremist groups and had provided information to the FBI.

The indictment represents a significant escalation in the Trump administration's legal and political campaign against left-leaning organizations. Blanche said the investigation had been underway for an extended period but was halted during the Biden administration before being revived under President Trump's second term. FBI Director Patel severed the FBI's working relationship with the SPLC in October, calling it a "partisan smear machine." The SPLC, founded in the early 1970s to defend the legal rights of Black Americans, has long drawn criticism from conservative figures for its practice of labeling far-right entities as hate groups.

The case will proceed in federal court in the Middle District of Alabama. The SPLC said it intends to vigorously defend itself against the charges. Legal and civil liberties advocates are expected to scrutinize the prosecution closely, given the broader pattern of the Trump administration using federal law enforcement against organizations that have been critical of its policies or officials.
